title: Needle-felted cactus set
subtitle: A trio of small cacti in terracotta pots, built from compacted green merino
excerpt: Needle-felt three compact cactus shapes in shades of green merino, add surface spine details with a fine needle, and seat them in small terracotta pots with a gravel top-dressing. A practical project for learning how to control needle-felted surface texture and to work small vertical forms.
sourceNotes: Needle-felted botanical figures are an established form in contemporary craft. These cactus shapes draw on standard column and sphere construction methods taught across guild workshops.

glossaryTerms.definition: A cylindrical needle-felted base shape built by rolling roving tightly and needling it from all sides to compact it into a firm upright column.
A fine line or dot of contrasting roving worked into the surface of a needle-felted piece using a very fine needle, creating the raised texture of cactus spines or other fine surface detail.

content.text: or a rounded sphere, needled firm in mid-green or blue-green merino, then given

lines in pale cream or white. The finished set works in terracotta pots 4-6 cm across. Filling the base of each pot with a small wad of plain roving before inserting the cactus keeps the piece upright.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. The needle must enter foam, not a hard surface, on every stroke.

items.name: Foam felting mat, at least 5 cm thick

content.text: Step 1: Build the cactus base shapes

For a column cactus: pull a 10 g length of mid-green roving, roll it tightly along its length into a cylinder about 6 cm tall and 2 cm across. Needle all sides with a size 38 needle until the cylinder holds its shape without unrolling. Work from top to bottom, rotating the form a quarter-turn after every ten strokes, until the surface is firm and the cylinder stands unsupported.

For a rounded cactus: gather 8 g of blue-green roving into a tight ball and needle it on the mat, turning after each series of strokes, until it compacts to a dense sphere roughly 4 cm across. Flatten the base slightly so it sits level.

Switch to the size 40 fine needle. Take a very small pinch of pale cream roving and lay it against the cactus surface to form a shallow V-shape (the spine cluster radiates from a single point). Needle the centre point of the V firmly to anchor it, then work outward along each arm. Repeat at regular intervals around the form. For the column, work in horizontal rows spaced about 1 cm apart.

Press a wad of plain dark-grey or terracotta-coloured roving into the base of each pot to act as ballast. Insert the finished cactus form, pressing the base firmly into the roving. Top-dress with fine gravel or sand to cover the roving and suggest a soil surface.

items.problem: The column form buckles or leans
items.cause: The base was not needled firm enough before the top was worked
items.fix: Return to the base and work additional strokes from bottom to mid-point until the lower third is dense. Prop upright against a rolled cloth while adding the top section.

items.problem: Spine lines sink into the surface and disappear
items.cause: Too much pressure on the fine needle, driving the spine fibres too deep
items.fix: Use the finest needle available and use lighter, shallower strokes. The spine fibres need to lock at the surface, not penetrate the base.

items.problem: The cactus wobbles in the pot
items.cause: Insufficient ballast roving in the pot base
items.fix: Pack more roving into the pot and needle it lightly to compress it, then re-insert the cactus and press it down firmly.
